• 
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Raymond T. Littrell 
Councilman, city of Garden Grove 
P.O. Box 3070 
Garden Grove, CA 
92642 
Dear Councilman Littrell: 
August 17, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-88-310 
Chairman Larson asked me to respond to your letter of 
August 5, 1988, in which you make suggestions for a revision to the 
Form 470 (Candidate and Officeholder campaign statement - Short 
Form) . 
We have received other questions and concerns about the 
Form 470, and will attempt to clarify the circumstances under which 
it should be used by candidates and officeholders. 
The provision for use of a "short form" by candidates and 
officeholders is contained in Government Code section 84206 which 
provides that it may be used by: 
candidates and officeholders who receive 
contributions of less than one thousand dollars 
($1,000), and who make expenditures of less than 
one thousand dollars ($1,000), in a calendar year. 
I believe the confusion concerning use of the short form 
arises from the fact that all candidates and officeholders, whether 
or not they have any campaign activity, are required to file certain 
campaign statements (pre-election or semi-annual statements) prior 
to the end of the year. 
And, as you point out, they do not know for 
sure until the end of the year whether they will actually raise or 
spend $1,000 or more. 
The staff has always advised candidates and 
officeholders that they may use the Form 470 if they anticipate that 
they will raise less than $1,000 and .will spend less than $1,000 
during the entire calendar year. 
We will review the use of the Form 470, particularly the 
language of the verification. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804~0807 • (916) 322~5660 


• 
Raymond T. Littrell 
August 17, 1988 
Page Two 
Thank you for taking the time to write to us about this 
problem. 
We appreciate your suggestions. 
Sincerely, C.

Technical Assistance and 
Analysis Division 


1 1 3 9 1 A CAe I APR K WAY. P O. BOX 3 0 7 O. GAR DEN G ROE, C A 
I FOR N I A 9 2 6 4 2 
August 5, 1988 
John Larson, Chairman 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 IIJII Street 
Post Office Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804-0807 
Re: 
Form 470 Candidate and Officeholder Campaign Statement 
Dear Mr. Larson: 
I am writing you to ask the Commission to review and revise Form 470 and 
the accompanying cover sheet. 
Form 470 is a problem for elected offi-
ci a 1 s because it ca 11 s for report ing what has happened and what mayor 
may not happen at the same level of certainty. 
The problem arises at the 
time of midyear fi 1 ings of Form 470. 
The fi 1 ing party must report cam-
paign contributions for the first six months of the year. 
This is a fair 
requirement. 
Also, the filing party must report the expectancy of no or 
limited campaign contributions for the last six months of the year as a 
certainty lIunder penalty of perjury." This is not fair. 
Often decisions 
to file for election for offices submitted to the electorate in the Fall 
or, even the Spring, are made after the midyear filing deadline in mid-
summer or in the Fall. 
An elected official's "guess" is reported as a 
"fact," even if that elected official knows his or her circumstances 
could change. 
We have been informed by Kathryn Donovan that the FPPC has had other com-
plaints about this problem with Form 470. 
She stated that the FPPC 
advice to elected officials ;s "use your best judgment at the time of 
filing, and file a 110ng form l 
campaign statement if plans change and 
contributions are received or solicited.
1I 
The FPPC position is fair and 
eliminates the risk of penalties. 
However, the political risk to an 
elected official remains. 
A member of the public or an opposition candi-
date can obtain the midyear filing and use it for misleading pol; cal 
For 
le, the elected offi 
a1 could 
accused of lyi 
1 tical ions 
purposes 
, I would 
at 
ver fi-
be changed to 


John Larson 
August 5, 1988 
Page 2 
Re: 
Form 470 Candidate and Officeholder Campaign Statement 
"I declare under penalty of perjury that to the best 
of my knowledge, during the calendar year, I have not 
yet rece i ved or expended $1, 000 and am not present ly 
actively soliciting campaign contributions, and that I 
have used all reasonable diligence in preparing this 
statement. II 
This change would 
allow 
elected official to discuss political 
options, so long as campaign contributions are not being solicited. 
T. 
Councilman

California

Practices Commission 
Honorable Raymond T. Littrell 
councilmember 
city of Garden Grove 
P.O. Box 3070 
Garden Grove, CA 
92642 
Dear Mr. Littrell: 
August 10, 1988 
Re: 
88-310 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on August 8, 1988 by the Fair Political 
Practices commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5662. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to the information needed. 
If your request is 
for informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we 
can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. 
Sec. 18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
JP:plh 
Very truly yours,

1988. 
FORM 470 
CANDIDATE AND OFFICEHOLDER CAMPAIGN STATEMENT -- SHORT FORM 
• 
FOR 
USE 
BY CANDIDATES AND OFFICEHOLDERS VI/HO DO NOT HAVE A CONTROLLED COMMITTEE 
AND WHO WILL NOT RECEIVE $1,000 OR MORE AND WILL NOT SPEND $1,000 OR MORE DURING 
THE ENTiRE CALENDAR YEAR. 
• 
THE PERIOD COVERED IS ALWAYS THE CALENDAR YEAR (JANUARY 1 THROUGH DECEMBER 31). 
• 
TO DETERMINE IF $1,000 HAS BEEN RAISED OR SPENT OR W 
BE 
RAISED OR SPENT, THE 
CANDIDATE'S PERSONAL FUNDS FOR THE FILING 
FEE OR STATE MY ,T OF QUALIFICATIONS ARE 
EXCLUDED. 
HOWEVER, SUCH PAYMENTS ARE "EXPENDITURES" AN 
ARE REPORTABLE IF THE 
CANDIDATE IS SUBSEQUENTLY REQUIRED TO FILE A CAMPAIGN STAT 
ENT LONG FORM (FORM 
490). IN ADDITION, CONTRIBUTIONS MADE TO OTHER CANDIDATES, OFFICEHOLDERS AND COMMITTEES 
FROM A CANDIDATE'S OR OFFICEHOLDER'S PERSONAL FUNDS OR THE PERSONAL FUNDS OF HIS OR HER 
SPOUSE ARE INCLUDED FOR PURPOSES OF THE $1,000 THRESHOLD. 
• 
IF THE 
FORM 470 
IS 
FILED BY A CANDIDATE OR OFFICEHOLDER WITH A 
DECLARATION OF 
CANDIDACY, OR ON OR BEFORE THE FILING DEADLINE FOR THE FIRST CAMPAIGN STATEMENT 
REQUIRED FOR THE CURRENT CALENDAR YEAR, NO ADDITIONAL CAMPAIGN STATEMENTS NEED BE 
FILED FOR THAT CALENDAR YEAR SO LONG AS TOTAL RECEIPTS AND TOTAL EXPENDITURES REMAIN 
LESS 
THAN $1,000. 
(IN MOST CASES, THE FILING DEADLINE FOR THE FIRST CAMPAIGN STATEMENT 
REQUIRED TO BE FILED BY OFFICEHOLDERS AND CANDIDATES NOT BEING VOTED UPON IS JULY 31. SEE 
THE "INFORMATION MANUAL ON CAMPAIGN DISCLOSURE PROVISIONS OF THE POLITICAL REFORM ACT" 
FOR FURTHER INFORMATION.) 
IMPORTANT 
"COMMITTEES" MUST FILE SEMI-ANNUAL CAMPAIGN DISCLOSURE REPORTS FOR EACH SIX-
MONTH PERIOD WHETHER OR NOT THEY HAD ANY ACTIVITY DURING THE SIX-MONTH PERIOD 
COVERED BY THE STATEMENT. THEREFORE, OFFICEHOLDERS AND CANDIDATES WHO HAVE A 
"CONTROLLED COMMITTEE- SHOULD FILE THE FORM 490, CANDIDATE AND OFFICEHOLDER 
CAMPAIGN STATEMENT-LONG FORM AND CONSOLIDATED CAMPAIGN STATEMENT. 
OFFICEHOLDERS WHOSE SALARY IS LESS THAN $100 PER MONTH AND JUDGES, WHO HAVE A 
CONTROLLED COMMITTEE, SHOULD REFER TO THE "INFORMATION MANUAL ON CAMPAIGN 
DISCLOSURE PROVISIONS OF THE POLITICAL REFORM ACT," TO DETERMINE THEIR FILING 
OBLIGATIONS.

use this form under certain circumstances. See the "Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions of 
the Pol itical Reform Act, N for further information. 
VERIFICATION 
I declare under penalty of perjury that to the best of my knowledge, during the calendar year, less than $1,000 
will be received and less than $1,000 will be expended by myself and that I have used all reasonable diligence in 
preparing this statement. 
I certify under penalty of perjury under the laws of the State of California that the foregoing is true and 
correct

sections.question: The filing party must report campaign contributions for the first six months of the year. Also, the filing party must report the expectancy of no or limited campaign contributions for the last six months of the year as a certainty 'under penalty of perjury.' This is not fair. Often decisions to file for election for offices submitted to the electorate in the Fall or, even the Spring, are made after the midyear filing deadline in mid-summer or in the Fall. An elected official's 'guess' is reported as a 'fact,' even if that elected official knows his or her circumstances could change. I would like the verification language to be changed to: 'I declare under penalty of perjury that to the best of my knowledge, during the calendar year, I have not yet received or expended $1,000 and am not presently actively soliciting campaign contributions, and that I have used all reasonable diligence in preparing this statement.'
sections.conclusion: I believe the confusion concerning use of the short form arises from the fact that all candidates and officeholders, whether or not they have any campaign activity, are required to file certain campaign statements (pre-election or semi-annual statements) prior to the end of the year. And, as you point out, they do not know for sure until the end of the year whether they will actually raise or spend $1,000 or more. The staff has always advised candidates and officeholders that they may use the Form 470 if they anticipate that they will raise less than $1,000 and will spend less than $1,000 during the entire calendar year. We will review the use of the Form 470, particularly the language of the verification.

sections.question_synthetic: Should the FPPC revise Form 470's verification language to allow elected officials to distinguish between what has already occurred and what may occur in the future, particularly to avoid penalizing elected officials who cannot know for certain at mid-year filing whether they will receive or spend $1,000 or more by year-end?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC acknowledges the ambiguity in Form 470 arising from uncertainty at mid-year filing about whether thresholds will be met by year-end, explains its current guidance to use best judgment, and commits to reviewing the form and its verification language.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This is a complete advice letter file containing the incoming request (August 5, 1988) from Littrell, an acknowledgment letter (August 10, 1988) from FPPC, and the substantive response (August 17, 1988) from Jeanne Pritchard. The question is paraphrased from Littrell's detailed narrative argument. The conclusion includes FPPC's commitments rather than a definitive ruling. OCR quality is good overall.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The filing party must report campaign contributions for the first six months of the year. Also, the filing party must report the expectancy of no or limited campaign contributions for the last six months of the year as a certainty 'under penalty of perjury.' This is not fair. Often decisions to file for election for offices submitted to the electorate in the Fall or, even the Spring, are made after the midyear filing deadline in mid-summer or in the Fall. An elected official's 'guess' is reported as a 'fact,' even if that elected official knows his or her circumstances could change. I would like the verification language to be changed to: 'I declare under penalty of perjury that to the best of my knowledge, during the calendar year, I have not yet received or expended $1,000 and am not presently actively soliciting campaign contributions, and that I have used all reasonable diligence in preparing this statement.'

CONCLUSION: I believe the confusion concerning use of the short form arises from the fact that all candidates and officeholders, whether or not they have any campaign activity, are required to file certain campaign statements (pre-election or semi-annual statements) prior to the end of the year. And, as you point out, they do not know for sure until the end of the year whether they will actually raise or spend $1,000 or more. The staff has always advised candidates and officeholders that they may use the Form 470 if they anticipate that they will raise less than $1,000 and will spend less than $1,000 during the entire calendar year. We will review the use of the Form 470, particularly the language of the verification.

We have received other questions and concerns about the Form 470, and will attempt to clarify the circumstances under which it should be used by candidates and officeholders. The provision for use of a "short form" by candidates and officeholders is contained in Government Code section 84206 which provides that it may be used by: candidates and officeholders who receive contributions of less than one thousand dollars ($1,000), and who make expenditures of less than one thousand dollars ($1,000), in a calendar year. I believe the confusion concerning use of the short form arises from the fact that all candidates and officeholders, whether or not they have any campaign activity, are required to file certain campaign statements (pre-election or semi-annual statements) prior to the end of the year. And, as you point out, they do not know for sure until the end of the year whether they will actually raise or spend $1,000 or more. The staff has always advised candidates and officeholders that they may use the Form 470 if they anticipate that they will raise less than $1,000 and .will spend less than $1,000 during the entire calendar year. embedding.summary: Councilman Littrell requested FPPC review and revision of Form 470's verification language because it conflates reported past contributions/expenditures with uncertain future projections under penalty of perjury. The FPPC responded by acknowledging the confusion, explaining current guidance allows use of best judgment, and committing to review the form's verification language.
